1: Eikhah yashvah vadad ha-ir -- 'How the city sits alone!' The book opens with eikhah (H349), the particle of lamentation -- 'How!' or 'Alas!' -- used also in Isaiah 1:21 ('How the faithful city has become a harlot!') and Jeremiah 48:17. It is also the book's Hebrew name. The word is not a question but an exclamation of grief at reversal: the formerly great city now sits vadad (alone, isolated, desolate). Rabati am hayetah ke-almanah -- 'she who was great among the nations has become like a widow.' The widow (almanah) is the most vulnerable social category in ancient Israel -- without male protection, without status, without provision. Jerusalem's greatness has been stripped down to this condition. The verse initiates the first aleph-bet acrostic (aleph = the first letter).
2: Bakho tivkeh ba-layil -- 'She weeps bitterly in the night.' The infinitive absolute bakho before the finite verb tivkeh intensifies: she weeps and weeps, continuously and desperately. Ve-dimatah al lhyah -- 'and her tears are on her cheeks.' The tears flow without being wiped away -- the image of grief so deep it goes unattended. Ein-lah menachem mi-kol-ohaveyha -- 'she has no comforter from all her lovers.' The first occurrence of the menachem (H5162, comforter) refrain that dominates Lamentations 1. The 'lovers' (ohavim) are the political allies -- Egypt, Assyria, the surrounding nations -- who promised protection but have abandoned her. Kol-reyha bagdu vah -- 'all her friends have dealt treacherously with her' -- bagad (to betray/act treacherously), the vocabulary of covenant betrayal.
3: Galtah Yehudah me-oni u-me-rov avodah -- 'Judah has gone into exile because of affliction and because of great servitude.' Galah (H1540, to go into exile/uncover) -- the displacement of the covenant people from their land. Oni (H6040, affliction/misery) and avodah (H5656, labor/servitude) are the conditions that drove the exile. Hi yoshevet baggoyim lo matzaah manoach -- 'she dwells among the nations; she finds no resting place.' Manoach (resting place) -- the same word used in Ruth 3:1 for the security of marriage-home; Judah in exile has no such rest. Kol-rodfeha hisiguha bein ha-metzarim -- 'all her pursuers overtook her in the narrow places.' Metzarim (narrow straits/distress) -- also the word for Egypt (Mitzrayim); the exile involves a reversal to Egypt-like bondage.
4: Darkei Tziyyon avelot -- 'The roads of Zion mourn.' The personification extends: the roads themselves grieve because no pilgrim traffic moves on them. Mi-bli vai moed -- 'because none come to the appointed feast.' Moed (H4150, appointed time/feast/assembly) -- the great pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Shavuot, Sukkot) that filled the roads with worshippers have ceased. Kol-sharehah shomemot -- 'all her gates are desolate.' Shomemot (desolate/appalled) -- the verb used for the desolation of the Temple in Daniel 9:27. Kohanehah neenaichim -- 'her priests groan.' The priests who led the cult now lead the grief. Betulotehah nugot ve-hi mar-lah -- 'her virgins are afflicted and she herself is bitter.' Betulot (young women/virgins) -- the most vulnerable of the population, representing both future hope and present desolation.
5: Hayu tzarehah le-rosh -- 'Her enemies have become the head.' Tzarehah (her enemies/adversaries) has inverted the covenant promise of Deuteronomy 28:44 -- the covenant curse is that the enemy will be the head and Israel the tail when Israel disobeys. Oyvehah shalu -- 'her foes prosper.' Shavah (to prosper/be at peace) -- the peace that was Israel's covenant gift now belongs to the oppressor. Ki-YHWH hogah al rov-pishaeyha -- 'because YHWH has afflicted her for the abundance of her transgressions.' Hogah (to afflict/grieve, H3013) -- the divine action is named clearly: the enemy's victory is YHWH's instrument. Pishaeyha (her transgressions, H6588) -- covenant violations, not merely moral failures. Olaleyha halekhu shevi lifnei tzar -- 'her children have gone into captivity before the enemy.' The children in exile is the sharpest image of total loss.
6: Vayetze mi-bat-Tziyyon kol-hadarah -- 'And gone from Daughter Zion is all her majesty.' Hadar (H1926, majesty/splendor/honor) -- the royal dignity and sacred glory associated with Jerusalem and the Temple. Its departure is the inverse of the kavod YHWH (glory of YHWH) departing the Temple in Ezekiel 10-11. Hayu sareha ke-ayalim lo matzeu mirheh -- 'her princes have become like deer that find no pasture.' The simile: deer (ayalim) without pasture are desperate, directionless, and doomed. Princes who should lead are now like animals fleeing a wasteland. Vayelekhu velo-koach lifnei rodeif -- 'and they fled without strength before the pursuer.' The exhaustion of total defeat.
7: Zakhrah Yerushalayim yemei onyah u-merudeha -- 'Jerusalem remembers in the days of her affliction and her wandering.' Zekhirah (H2142, to remember) -- memory is both gift and torment: she remembers what she has lost. Kol machamudeha asher hayu mi-yemei qedem -- 'all the precious things she had from days of old.' Machmudim (H4261, precious/desirable things) -- the Temple treasures, the liturgy, the peace. Bi-nefol ammah be-yad tzar ve-ein ozer lah -- 'when her people fell into the hand of the enemy and there was no helper.' Ozer (H5826, helper) -- another dimension of the abandoned woman; she cries out and no one answers. Rauha tzarim sachaku al-mishbateyha -- 'the enemies looked on her and mocked at her downfall.' The public shame of defeated Jerusalem witnessed by her captors.
8: Chet chatah Yerushalayim -- 'Jerusalem has sinned greatly.' The doubled construction (cognate accusative) intensifies: she has sinned a sin, she has grossly transgressed. Al-ken le-nidah hayetah -- 'therefore she has become an object of abhorrence.' Niddah (H5079, menstrual impurity/thing to be put away) -- the ritual category of separation. Jerusalem's sin has rendered her ritually impure and therefore socially excluded, the opposite of the holy city at the center of the nations. Kol-mechabbedehah hizziluha -- 'all who honored her despise her, for they have seen her nakedness.' The reversal: those who gave honor now give contempt. Ervah (H6172, nakedness) -- exposure and shame; also the legal language of sexual scandal and covenant violation (Deuteronomy 24:1).
9: Tumah be-shulehah -- 'Her uncleanness was in her skirts.' Tumah (H2932, impurity/uncleanness) -- ceremonial defilement. The metaphor of pollution in the hem of the garment represents the accumulation of sin that had become visible but unaddressed. Lo zakhrah acharitah -- 'she did not consider her end.' Acharit (H319, end/latter part/outcome) -- the same word Proverbs uses for wisdom's foresight: the fool does not consider how things will end. Jerusalem lacked the wisdom to foresee the consequences of covenant violation. Vattered plaim ein menachem lah -- 'she has fallen terribly; there is no one to comfort her.' The second menachem refrain. Reh YHWH et-onyah -- 'Look, O YHWH, at my affliction!' The first direct address to YHWH, breaking from third-person description to first-person appeal.
10: Yado parash tzar -- 'The enemy spread out his hand.' Parash yad (to spread the hand) -- the gesture of plundering; the enemy's hand extended over Jerusalem's treasuries. Al kol-machamudeha -- 'over all her precious things.' The machmudim return from Lamentations 1:7 -- Temple vessels, sacred objects, royal treasuries -- all now handled by the enemy's plundering hand. Ki ratah goyim bau mikdashah -- 'for she saw the nations enter her sanctuary.' The ultimate desecration: Gentiles entering the qodesh (holy place) that was forbidden even to many Israelites. Asher tzivvitah lo-yavou vaqahal lakh -- 'those whom you commanded not to enter your assembly.' The Torah's exclusion of certain nations from the assembly (Deuteronomy 23:2-9) has been violated by the very fact of conquest.
11: Kol-ammah neenaichim -- 'All her people groan.' Neanom (H584, to sigh/groan) -- the sound of desperate need, the moan that precedes prayer. Mevaqshim lachem -- 'seeking bread.' The famine that accompanied the siege (2 Kings 25:3; Jeremiah 52:6) -- the most basic human need unmet. Natenu machamudehem be-okhel le-hashiv nafesh -- 'they give their precious things for food to revive their strength.' The machmudim (precious things) appear a third time -- now traded for survival food. What was sacred becomes mere barter. Re eh YHWH ve-habbitah ki hayiti zelulaah -- 'Look, O YHWH, and see, for I have become worthless.' The second direct address to YHWH -- the cry intensifies.
12: Lo aleikhem kol-ovrei derekh -- 'Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by?' The most famous verse in Lamentations. The Hebrew lo aleikhem (not to you) is literally: 'nothing to you?' -- a challenge to the indifferent passerby. Jerusalem appeals to witnesses. Habitu u-reu im-yesh makhov ke-makhovi asher olal li -- 'Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow, which was brought upon me.' Makhov (H4341, pain/sorrow) -- the word for grief that goes to the depths of one's being. The claim is that Jerusalem's suffering is unique in its severity. Asher hoga YHWH be-yom charon appo -- 'which YHWH inflicted on the day of his fierce anger.' The divine agency is again named; the suffering is not random but the result of YHWH's furious, purposeful action.
13: Mi-marom shalach-esh be-atzmotai -- 'From on high he sent fire into my bones.' The divine fire is not metaphorical -- it describes the destruction's violence reaching into the marrow of Jerusalem's life. Atzmot (bones) -- frequently used for the essential core of one's being (Psalms 6:3; Lamentations 31:11; Ezek 37). The fire from above (YHWH) burned downward into the inmost structure of the city and people. Vayirdenah -- 'and it subdued them.' Peras reshet le-raglai -- 'He spread a net for my feet.' The net imagery (H7568) -- used of hunters trapping prey; YHWH as the one who set the trap for Jerusalem's escape. Heshipani achoranit -- 'he turned me back.' Natani shomemah kol-hayom dava -- 'he has made me desolate and faint all the day long.'
14: Nishqad ol pashai be-yado -- 'The yoke of my transgressions was bound.' Nishqad (to bind tightly/to be wakeful over) -- an unusual verb suggesting careful, deliberate binding. Pesha (H6588, transgression) -- the same strong covenant-violation term as Lamentations 1:5. The transgressions are not loose moral failures but a yoke -- ol (H5923) -- bound onto Jerusalem's neck by YHWH's own hand. Yishtargu alu al-tzavvari -- 'they are woven together; they go up upon my neck.' The intertwining of the sins into a yoke suggests their accumulated, integrated character -- not isolated acts but a pattern of covenant violation. Hikhshil kochi -- 'he has made my strength fail.' The inevitable result of bearing the yoke of judgment.
15: Sillah kol-abirai YHWH be-kirbi -- 'The Lord rejected all my mighty men in my midst.' Sillah (H5541, to set aside/toss away) -- a contemptuous discarding. The military strength of Jerusalem -- its elite warriors -- was cast aside by YHWH as ineffective. Qara alai moed -- 'he summoned an assembly against me.' The tragic irony: moed normally denotes the sacred festival assembly of worship; here YHWH calls a moed against Jerusalem -- a military assembly of destruction rather than a liturgical assembly of praise. Lishbor bachurai -- 'to crush my young men.' The youth who should have been the future were crushed. Gat darak YHWH le-vetulat bat-Yehudah -- 'The Lord has trodden the virgin Daughter Judah as in a winepress.' The winepress image (gat) -- harvest fruit crushed underfoot -- applied to the people of Judah. A reversal of vineyard imagery from Isaiah.
16: Al-elleh ani vokhiyah -- 'For these things I weep.' The first-person voice of the personified city intensifies. Eini eini yeredah mayim -- 'my eye, my eye runs down with water.' The doubled eini emphasizes the uncontrollable flow of tears. Ki rachaq mimmenni menachem -- 'because far from me is the comforter.' The third menachem refrain -- but now the comforter's absence is described spatially: rachaq (far, distant). The one who should be near is unreachably far. Meshiv nafshi -- the comforter who would 'restore my soul' (same phrase as Psalms 23:3 -- YHWH the shepherd restoring his flock). Hayu vanai shomemim ki gavar ha-oyev -- 'my children are desolate because the enemy has prevailed.'
17: Pershah Tziyyon et-yadehah ein menachem lah -- 'Zion spreads out her hands; there is no comforter for her.' The fourth and climactic menachem refrain in Lamentations 1. Pershah yadehah -- the gesture of prayer and petition, hands spread upward or outward in appeal. But the hands reach into emptiness; no comforter responds. Tzivvah YHWH le-Yaaqov sevivav tzarav -- 'YHWH has commanded Jacob's neighbors to be his enemies.' The theological weight: YHWH himself commanded the surrounding nations to become adversaries. The enemies are instruments of divine judgment, not autonomous aggressors. Hayetah Yerushalayim le-niddah beinetam -- 'Jerusalem has become an unclean thing among them.' The niddah exclusion of Lamentations 1:8 returns -- Jerusalem is quarantined from the community of nations by her ritual defilement of sin.
18: Tzaddiq hu YHWH -- 'The LORD is righteous.' In the middle of the lament, the city acknowledges divine justice: the destruction is not unjust. Ki fi mariti -- 'for I rebelled against his word.' Mariti (H4784, to rebel/be contentious) -- the admission of covenant rebellion. This verse is the theological hinge of Lamentations 1: Jerusalem's theological confession within the lament. She does not protest her innocence but admits her guilt. Shimu-na kol-ha-ammim ve-reu makhovi -- 'Hear, all peoples, and behold my sorrow.' The appeal to witness expands beyond passersby (Lamentations 1:12) to all nations. Betulotai u-vachurei halekhu vashevi -- 'my young women and young men have gone into captivity.' The youth -- the future -- in exile.
19: Qarati le-meahavai hemah rimmuni -- 'I called to my lovers; they deceived me.' Rimmuni (H7411, deceived/betrayed) -- the political allies who promised security have proved false. Kohanai u-zeqenai ba-ir gave u -- 'my priests and elders perished in the city.' Those who were meant to intercede (priests) and to counsel (elders) are dead. The institutional leadership has collapsed. Ki viqqeshu okhel lamo ve-yashivu et-nafesham -- 'while they sought food to revive their strength.' Even the priests died in the siege-famine, searching for bread like everyone else. The liturgical community that sustained the nation's covenant life has dissolved.
20: Reh YHWH ki tzar li -- 'Look, O YHWH, for I am in distress.' The third direct address to YHWH. Tzar (H6862, narrow/distress) -- the same root as metzarim (Lamentations 1:3) -- constriction, the opposite of the spaciousness of covenant blessing. Meai chamaru -- 'my stomach churns.' Chamaru (H2560, to ferment/churn) -- the internal upheaval of grief and fear. Nefekh libbi alai -- 'my heart is overturned within me.' Nafakh (to be overturned/poured out) -- the dissolution of inner structure. Ki marod mariti -- 'for I have been very rebellious.' The repeated confession: twice in this chapter Jerusalem names her own rebellion as the cause. Mi-chutz shikhkhelah cherev ba-bayit ka-mavet -- 'Outside, the sword bereaves; inside, it is like death.' The siege has made outside and inside equally fatal.
21: Shamu ki neenaichti -- 'They heard how I am groaning.' Neanaichti (H584, I am groaning) -- the groan as both audible suffering and implicit prayer. Ein menachem li -- the fifth and final menachem refrain of Lamentations 1. The entire first chapter is structured around this absence: the comforter who does not come. Kol-oyvai shameu raati sasu -- 'all my enemies heard of my trouble; they rejoiced.' Sasu (rejoiced/exulted) -- the enemy's delight at Jerusalem's fall, which will itself become a subject of lamentation and judgment. Heveita yom-qarata -- 'You brought on the day you announced.' The enemies' joy is within YHWH's announced plan.
22: Tavo kol-raatam lefanekha -- 'Let all their evil come before you.' The lament turns to imprecation -- a prayer for YHWH to deal with the enemies as he has dealt with Jerusalem. Ve-olel lamo ka-asher olalta li -- 'and deal with them as you have dealt with me for all my transgressions.' The final verse requests proportionate divine justice: the enemies, who have also transgressed, should face the same judgment Jerusalem has faced. Ki rabbim anachoti ve-libbi davvai -- 'for my groans are many and my heart is faint.' The book's first chapter ends not with resolution but with ongoing grief: the groaning continues, the heart remains sick. The lack of comforter with which the chapter opened is matched by the lack of comfort with which it closes -- an unresolved lament awaiting divine response.
